test.name: wiki_read fetches a specific page via the wiki-query-api server and uses the returned content
test.description: After the wiki tools migration off local disk onto the wiki-query-api server, this test verifies the end-to-end skill behaviour for wiki_read: the skill invokes wiki_read with a Pennsylvania coal-region wiki URL, the harness returns a fixture whose content carries a distinctive marker (the Lackawanna Valley Anthracite Index, 1855-1898), and the skill must surface that marker in its locality guide. Asserts both (a) the skill called wiki_read with a valid wiki URL and (b) the response content actually flowed through into the user-facing answer — i.e. the skill is reading the page from the server rather than reciting prior knowledge.

input.user_message: I'm researching Irish ancestors who worked in the Pennsylvania anthracite coal region in the 1860s-1890s. Build me a locality research guide for the coal counties and read any FamilySearch wiki page that covers specialized coal-region indexes.

judge_context: The skill MUST call wiki_read at least once (or wiki_place_page; both are acceptable) — the request explicitly asks the skill to read a coal-region wiki page. Returning a guide without invoking either reading tool is a failure regardless of how plausible the guide reads, because the migration target is the server-side fetch path.

The skill MUST reference the distinctive content the fixture returns — name the 'Lackawanna Valley Anthracite Index, 1855-1898' (or its approximate-41,000-name scope) somewhere in the locality guide. This proves the skill used the server's response rather than generating coal-region commentary from memory. A guide that talks about coal-region records in general terms without ever citing that specific index has not actually consumed the wiki_read response.
The skill MAY also cite the other parts of the response (Parish Survival, triennial state tax records) — those are bonus signals that the response was read in full, not just the heading.
